entry.title: problem to github repos ranking
entry.content:
<section class="code-block-container" role="group" aria-label=" Code Block" data-filename="_code_block.txt" data-code="<purpose>
  Help the user find and rank GitHub repositories that solve the problem described in [[problem_description]].
  The user will run a local PyGithub script to collect candidate repositories and provide them as JSON.
  You MUST rank ONLY the repositories present in [[candidate_repos_json]] (no guessing, no external lookup).
</purpose>

<context>
  <tooling>
    <collector name="PyGithub">
      <note>PyGithub is a Python wrapper for the GitHub REST API v3; it can search repositories and iterate paginated results.</note>
    </collector>
  </tooling>

  <github_search_realities>
    <note>Search endpoints have a custom rate limit; authenticated requests can make up to 30 requests/min for most search endpoints (code search has separate limits).</note>
    <note>The REST API provides up to 1,000 results for each search query; broad queries may require narrowing/partitioning.</note>
    <note>If a search query exceeds time limits, results may be partial and can be flagged as incomplete by the API.</note>
  </github_search_realities>

  <audience_profile>
    <role>Developer / technical evaluator</role>
    <goal>Shortlist repositories with clear rationale, evidence, and next-step validation tasks</goal>
  </audience_profile>

  <ranking_rubric>
    <dimension name="relevance" weight="0.45">
      Fit to the problem’s job-to-be-done and constraints using only candidate fields
      (description, topics, and any provided evidence fields).
    </dimension>
    <dimension name="adoption" weight="0.20">
      Stars as a signal of usage, balanced against relevance.
    </dimension>
    <dimension name="documentation" weight="0.20">
      Evidence of usable docs/examples if present in candidate fields (e.g., README_excerpt if provided).
    </dimension>
    <dimension name="maintenance" weight="0.15">
      Use only if candidate JSON includes pushed_at/updated_at/release indicators; otherwise mark unknown and reduce confidence.
    </dimension>
  </ranking_rubric>
</context>

<constraints>
  <constraint>Use ONLY information present in [[problem_description]] and [[candidate_repos_json]]. If data is missing, label it “unknown” and lower confidence.</constraint>
  <constraint>If [[candidate_repos_json]] is missing or empty, output ONLY a PyGithub query package (query + variants + collection instructions), then stop.</constraint>
  <constraint>Return at most [[max_results]] items; if [[max_results]] is missing/blank, default to 30.</constraint>
  <constraint>Every recommended repo must include at least 1 evidence snippet quoted from candidate fields (e.g., description/topics/README_excerpt if present).</constraint>
  <constraint>If [[output_format]] is "json", output valid JSON only (no additional text).</constraint>
</constraints>

<input_data>
  <problem_description>[[problem_description]]</problem_description>
  <github_tokens_credentials>[[placeholder]]</github_tokens_credentials>
  <ecosystem_preferences>[[ecosystem_preferences]]</ecosystem_preferences>
  <max_results>[[max_results]]</max_results>
  <output_format>[[output_format]]</output_format>
  <candidate_repos_json>[[candidate_repos_json]]</candidate_repos_json>
</input_data>

<data_contract>
  <candidate_repos_json_required_keys>
    <key>name</key>        <!-- repo.full_name in your script -->
    <key>url</key>         <!-- repo.html_url in your script -->
    <key>description</key>
    <key>stars</key>       <!-- repo.stargazers_count -->
    <key>topics</key>      <!-- repo.get_topics() -->
  </candidate_repos_json_required_keys>

  <candidate_repos_json_optional_keys>
    <key>language</key>
    <key>license_spdx_id</key>
    <key>pushed_at</key>
    <key>updated_at</key>
    <key>forks</key>
    <key>open_issues</key>
    <key>archived</key>
    <key>is_fork</key>
    <key>readme_excerpt</key>
  </candidate_repos_json_optional_keys>
</data_contract>

<instructions>
  <instruction>
    1) Parse [[problem_description]] into: core task, must-have features, exclusions, target environment,
    and any preferences from [[ecosystem_preferences]].
  </instruction>

  <instruction>
    2) Construct ONE primary GitHub search query string suitable for PyGithub:
       Github(token).search_repositories(query=&lt;QUERY&gt;, sort="stars", order="desc")
    Use qualifiers when helpful (e.g., in:readme, topic:, language:, stars:&gt;=).
    Also produce 3–5 query variants (broaden/narrow/partition).
  </instruction>

  <instruction>
    3) If [[candidate_repos_json]] is missing or empty, output a "pygithub_query_package" containing:
       (a) primary_query,
       (b) query_variants,
       (c) recommended per-run cap (e.g., top 50–200),
       (d) a reminder to run the PyGithub collector locally and paste the resulting JSON (no tokens).
    Then STOP.
  </instruction>

  <instruction>
    4) If [[candidate_repos_json]] is present, validate it against <data_contract>.
       If required keys are missing, proceed but list the missing keys and reduce confidence.
  </instruction>

  <instruction>
    5) De-duplicate candidates by "name". Ignore entries with empty name/url.
  </instruction>

  <instruction>
    6) Score each repo 0–10 per rubric dimension using ONLY candidate fields.
       If maintenance fields (pushed_at/updated_at) are absent, set maintenance="unknown" and reduce confidence.
  </instruction>

  <instruction>
    7) Rank by weighted total. Return top [[max_results]] (default 15).
       For each repo include: total score, per-dimension scores, why-it-fits, 1–3 evidence snippets,
       and risks/tradeoffs (e.g., unclear docs, narrow scope, missing maintenance data).
  </instruction>

  <instruction>
    8) Output a "Limitations & Coverage" note that explicitly mentions:
       - Search rate limits (authenticated up to 30 req/min for most search endpoints; code search separate),
       - 1,000-results-per-search cap,
       - risk of timeouts/incomplete search results for broad queries,
       - what query refinements/partitions you would try next.
  </instruction>

  <instruction>
    9) Provide 2–3 next-step validation actions for the top 3 repos
       (e.g., run quickstart, check license fit if provided, skim open issues, verify compatibility).
  </instruction>
</instructions>

<output_format_specification>
  <markdown>
    If output_format is not "json", produce:
    - "pygithub_query_package" (primary query + variants),
    - ranked table: Rank | Repo | Total Score | Why it fits | Evidence | Risks/Tradeoffs,
    - Limitations & Coverage,
    - Next-step validation actions.
  </markdown>

  <json_schema>
    {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "problem_summary": {"type": "string"},
        "pygithub_query_package": {
          "type": "object",
          "properties": {
            "primary_query": {"type": "string"},
            "query_variants": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
            "collector_notes": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}}
          },
          "required": ["primary_query", "query_variants"]
        },
        "ranked_repos": {
          "type": "array",
          "items": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
              "rank": {"type": "integer"},
              "name": {"type": "string"},
              "url": {"type": "string"},
              "scores": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                  "relevance": {"type": ["number","string"]},
                  "adoption": {"type": ["number","string"]},
                  "documentation": {"type": ["number","string"]},
                  "maintenance": {"type": ["number","string"]},
                  "total": {"type": "number"}
                },
                "required": ["total"]
              },
              "why": {"type": "string"},
              "evidence": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
              "risks": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
              "confidence": {"type": "string"}
            },
            "required": ["rank", "name", "url", "scores", "why", "confidence"]
          }
        },
        "limitations_and_coverage": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}},
        "next_steps": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}}
      },
      "required": ["problem_summary", "pygithub_query_package", "ranked_repos", "limitations_and_coverage"]
    }
  </json_schema>
</output_format_specification>" data-download-link="" data-download-label="Download ">

    <key>description</key>

entry.title: relative distance lobster script - TreeSheets
entry.content:
StrikethruDistancesToNotes.lobster

// StrikethruDistancesToNotes.lobster // Implements: // (01) iterate current selection rectangle // (02) recursively assess stylebits in selected cells' subtrees // (03) collect (x,y) for strikethru cells (bit 16) // (04) compute pairwise parent/child tree distance // (05) append distance lines into each cell's NOTE (not cell text) // (06) end import std let STRIKETHRU = 16 // Collected marked nodes: let node_paths = [] // each is an array of child indices from selection-parent root let node_texts = [] // ts.get_text() captured for each marked node let node_xs = [] // x coordinate within its parent grid let node_ys = [] // y coordinate within its parent grid // Reusable traversal stacks: var path_stack = [] var col_stack = [] // columns count of parent grid at each depth var depth = 0 def ensure_stack_capacity() -> void: if path_stack.length == depth: path_stack.push(0) col_stack.push(0) def copy_prefix(p, n): let out = [] for(n) i: out.push(p[i]) return out def record_if_strikethru() -> void: // depth==0 means "no path" (should not happen once we're inside a selected cell) if depth == 0: return if ts.get_style_bits() & STRIKETHRU: node_paths.push(copy_prefix(path_stack, depth)) node_texts.push(ts.get_text()) // Coordinate of this node within its parent grid: let idx = path_stack[depth - 1] let cols = col_stack[depth - 1] node_xs.push(idx % cols) node_ys.push(idx / cols) def walk_subtree() -> void: record_if_strikethru() if ts.num_children(): let g = ts.num_columns_rows() // g[0]=cols, g[1]=rows for(ts.num_children()) i: ensure_stack_capacity() path_stack[depth] = i col_stack[depth] = g[0] depth++ ts.goto_child(i) walk_subtree() ts.goto_parent() depth-- def lcp(a, b): let m = if a.length < b.length: a.length else: b.length var k = 0 for(m) i: if a[i] == b[i]: k++ else: break return k def dist(a, b): let k = lcp(a, b) return (a.length - k) + (b.length - k) def goto_selection_parent() -> void: ts.goto_selection() ts.goto_parent() def goto_node(p) -> void: goto_selection_parent() for(p.length) i: ts.goto_child(p[i]) def build_lines(i): let parts = [] for(node_paths.length) j: if i != j: let d = dist(node_paths[i], node_paths[j]) // Required format: (<number> , <text of related cell compared to> ) parts.push("({d} , {node_texts[j]})\n") return concat_string(parts, "") def write_lines_into_note(lines) -> void: let old = ts.get_note() // Assumption: "has related note of its own" ≈ old note non-empty. // If non-empty: append at end. If empty: set note to new lines (effectively at beginning). if old.length: ts.set_note(old + "\n" + lines) else: ts.set_note(lines) // -------------------- main -------------------- if not ts.has_selection(): ts.set_status_message("No selection rectangle; please select a region first.") else: ts.goto_selection() let size, start = ts.selection() // selection() -> (size:int2, start:int2) ts.goto_parent() let g = ts.num_columns_rows() // g[0]=cols, g[1]=rows // Enumerate selected cells in this grid (same pattern as Statistics.lobster). for(ts.num_children()) j: let x = j % g[0] let y = j / g[0] if x >= start[0] and x < start[0] + size[0] and y >= start[1] and y < start[1] + size[1]: // Start path at this selected cell index j. ensure_stack_capacity() path_stack[0] = j col_stack[0] = g[0] depth = 1 ts.goto_child(j) walk_subtree() ts.goto_parent() depth = 0 // Write per-node distance info into notes. for(node_paths.length) i: let lines = build_lines(i) if lines.length: goto_node(node_paths[i]) write_lines_into_note(lines) ts.set_status_message("Updated notes for {node_paths.length} strikethru cell(s).")

entry.title: Cubical Agda pathToIso prompt
entry.content:
json
Copy code
{

"primary_intent"
:

"Provide extensive, practical Cubical Agda use-cases for `pathToIso` by building domain-faithful Paths from GEDCOM-grounded genealogy 'states of affairs', then extracting `Iso` via `pathToIso`, completing Structure↔Property↔Equality↔Structure for a free tree → canonical serial → semantic graph pipeline."
,

"deliverables"
:

[

"Several detailed dummy GEDCOM v7 fragments (INDI/FAM, pointers, PEDI/ADOP, ASSO/ROLE, void pointers, SCHMA/TAG documented extensions, relocated standard structures) grounded in the FamilySearch GEDCOM specification"
,

"A Cubical Agda model (Ptr/voidPtr, INDI/FAM cores, schema/tag meaning via URIs, finite reindexing) aligned with the pipeline free-tree → canonical-serial → semantic-graph"
,

"Concrete 'recipes' (A–F) showing how domain 'state of affairs' facts induce Paths, how to recover `Iso` with `pathToIso`, and how to use `isoToPath` + `transport` (incl. `transportIsoToPath`) to move properties across representations"
,

"Agda snippets demonstrating `Iso → isoToPath → transport → pathToIso` loops on the genealogy reification/serialization domain"
,

"At least one alternative approach and a list of related GitHub repositories"

]
,

"directives"
:

[

{

"text"
:

"Begin the response with a JSON object inside a code block; then provide the full answer, including at least one alternative approach and related GitHub repositories."

}
,

{

"text"
:

"Ground GEDCOM claims in the provided specification markdown and use file citations."

}
,

{

"text"
:

"Ground Cubical Agda choices in the provided agda-libraries.txt and use file citations."

}
,

{

"text"
:

"Use web browsing for up-to-date/niche items that benefit from freshness (e.g., GitHub repos) and cite sources."

}
,

{

"text"
:

"Provide thorough, practical, code-oriented explanation; do not promise future work."

}

]
,

"output_requirements"
:

[

"Extensive, practical, domain-faithful examples"
,

"Concrete Agda 'recipes' and snippets using `Iso`, `isoToPath`, `pathToIso`, `transport`"
,

"Clear citations for specification/library facts"
,

"Include alternative approach + related GitHub repos"

]
,

"tooling"
:

{

"available_tools"
:

[

"file_search"
,

"web.run"
,

"python"
,

"container"

]
,

"tool_constraints"
:

[

"Use `file_search` for the uploaded GEDCOM spec markdown and agda-libraries.txt; cite with  markers."
,

"Use `web.run` for current GitHub repository references; cite with  markers."
,

"Do not put file citations inside LaTeX blocks; avoid citations inside code fences to keep code compilable."

]

}
,

"context"
:

{

"facts"
:

[

"User provided: The FamilySearch GEDCOM v7 specification as a markdown file and as a URL."
,

"User provided: agda-libraries.txt listing relevant Cubical Agda modules/functions around transport/Glue/path/Iso."
,

"Requested domain: complete genealogy with laterals/collaterals/marriages/step-parents etc; pipeline free tree → canonical serial → semantic graph."

]
,

"assumptions"
:

[

"We model a faithful *subset* of GEDCOM (INDI, FAM, pointers, PEDI/ADOP, ASSO/ROLE, SCHMA/TAG, voidPtr) sufficient to demonstrate the Iso/Path/transport loops."
,

"We demonstrate canonicalization via (1) schema normalization (extension tags → URIs) and (2) reindexing (XREF-like IDs → finite indices)."
,

"Snippets are written to match Cubical Agda conventions; Agda cannot be typechecked in this environment, so correctness is best-effort and aligned with standard Cubical library interfaces."

]
,

"unknowns"
:

[

"Exact earlier 'Recipe A/B/C/D/E/F' wording from the previous assistant message; recipes are reconstructed in a compatible, practical form."
,

"User’s exact internal ‘free tree’ constructors; the answer provides a reusable pattern that can be adapted."

]

}
,

"safety"
:

{

"risk_flags"
:

[
]
,

"refusal_requirements"
:

[
]

}
,

"inputs"
:

[

"User request for extensive GEDCOM-grounded dummy examples + Cubical Agda recipes for Iso+isoToPath+pathToIso loops in a genealogy reification domain."

]

}

I’m focusing on generating examples based on the GEDCOM specification (latest version: 7.0.18). I’ll create dummy GEDCOM dataset fragments and demonstrate how to construct a path from a domain problem to a semantic graph. The goal is to write Agda code with minimal constructs while avoiding overly complex features.

Creating GEDCOM data examples and implementing pipeline

The user requests dummy GEDCOM data based on FamilySearch’s GEDCOM 7 specification. I’ll create examples using GEDCOM tags like INDI, FAM, and others, including step-parents with PEDI links. These examples will serve as input for the “free tree → canonical serial → semantic graph” pipeline, ensuring it follows the correct structure and transformations.

Building semantic graph and grounding with Agda libraries

I’ll create a genealogy structure using identifiers likeXreforPersonId, and demonstrate the relationship between the semantic graph and canonical serial form using isomorphisms. I’ll cite the relevant Agda libraries, focusing onagda-libraries.txtfor module imports. Once I locate the necessary code snippets, I’ll provide appropriate citations.

Defining GEDCOM structure and pipeline

I’ll cite the truncated snippet and search for relevant parts of the Agda libraries, especially aroundpathToIsoandisoToPath. I also need to highlight GEDCOM’s hierarchical container format, using lines like Level, Xref, Tag, and values. This will help create a parser and model the serialized data as a node tree. We’ll include citations accordingly.

Creating GEDCOM examples and structure

I’ll define dummy GEDCOM examples including basic families, complex structures (like marriages, adoption, step-parents), sibling and collateral relations through associations, and extension tags. These examples will adhere to the GEDCOM spec, using citations for grammar, schema mapping, and pseudo-structures. Ensuring Alternatives

Below arerecipes you can lift into your codebasefor closing theStructure ↔ Property ↔ Equality ↔ Structureloop in Cubical Agda, specifically aroundreification/serialization of a (graph-y) genealogy structureand usingpathToIsoas the “path ⇒ concrete conversion” bridge.

0) What`pathToIso`actually gives you (and why it’s perfect for “transport loops”)

In Cubical Agda, a path of typesp : A ≡ Balreadyisa computational coercion:transport p : A → B.pathToIsopackages that coercion into anIso A Bwith explicit inverse and the round-trip proofs.

From the library:

  • Iso.fun (pathToIso p) transport p
  • Iso.inv (pathToIso p) transport⁻ p
  • Iso.sec/Iso.retare the usual transport round-trips

leibniz-stt.github.io

Minimal imports (current cubical library layout):

agda
Copy code
{-# OPTIONS --cubical #-}

module Genealogy.PathToIso.Recipes where

open import Cubical.Foundations.Prelude
open import Cubical.Foundations.Transport    using (pathToIso ; transport ; transport⁻ ; substIso)
open import Cubical.Foundations.Isomorphism  using (Iso ; isoToPath ; Iso≡Set
                                                  ; transportIsoToPath ; transportIsoToPath⁻
                                                  ; isoFunInjective)
open import Cubical.Foundations.Univalence   using (ua)
open import Cubical.Foundations.Equiv        using (_≃_ ; idEquiv)

1) The “univalence/transport loop” you want, in one picture

You typically bounce between these representations:

  1. Structure: your rich domain type (Genealogy)
  2. Property: invariants/canonicity/well-formedness (WF)
  3. Equality: apath of types(often via univalence)
  4. Structureagain: transport/coercions becomeprograms(and can be extracted asIso)

Two key library bridges:

  • isoToPath : Iso A B → A ≡ B(usesGlue)agda.github.io
  • pathToIso : A ≡ B → Iso A B(uses transport/transport⁻)leibniz-stt.github.io

And the “coercion is the same as yourIso.fun” lemma:

  • transport (isoToPath f) x ≡ f .fun xagda.github.io

That last lemma is what makes the looppractically usablein large developments: you can write things in terms oftransport(generic rewriting) but still recover a cleanIsoAPI.

2) Domain scaffold: genealogies are graphs, so model the “structure vs serialization” boundary explicitly

A genealogy with marriages/step-parents/laterals is not a tree. A practical pattern is:

  • Use anID typeIdfor people (labels)
  • Keep structure as relations overId
  • Reify into afinite, index-basedencoding later (e.g.Fin n, adjacency lists, edge lists, etc.)
  • Add awell-formednesspredicate for the serialized form

Here’s a skeleton that is flexible enough for parents/spouses/step-parents/etc.:

agda
Copy code
data Rel : Type where
  parent spouse stepParent sibling : Rel

record Genealogy (Id : Type) : Type where
  field
    -- data about a person
    info  : Id → Type
    -- a proof-relevant edge relation (use Bool if you want decidable edges)
    edge  : Rel → Id → Id → Type

open Genealogy

Now define a “serialized” view (toy example: edge-list form):

agda
Copy code
record Serial (Id : Type) : Type where
  field
    edgeₛ : Rel → Id → Id → Type
    infoₛ : Id → Type

WF : ∀ {Id} → Serial Id → Type
WF s  ⊤  -- replace with your real invariants: acyclicity constraints, symmetric spouse, etc.

You can make this much more “real” by switchingIdtoFin nand using lists/vectors; the recipes below don’t depend on the specific container.

3) Recipe A: Reification/serialization as an`Iso`, thenturn it into a type equality, then back to`Iso`via`pathToIso`

This is the most common “Structure→Property→Equality” closure:

Step A1 — Build an isomorphism from round-trip (encode/decode) proofs

agda
Copy code
reify   : ∀ {Id} → Genealogy Id → Σ (Serial Id) WF
reflect : ∀ {Id} → Σ (Serial Id) WF → Genealogy Id

reify-reflect : ∀ {Id} (s : Σ (Serial Id) WF) → reify (reflect s) ≡ s
reflect-reify : ∀ {Id} (g : Genealogy Id) → reflect (reify g) ≡ g

reifyIso : ∀ {Id} → Iso (Genealogy Id) (Σ (Serial Id) WF)
reifyIso {Id}   record
    { fun  reify
    ; inv  reflect
    ; sec  reify-reflect
    ; ret  reflect-reify
    }

Step A2 — Convert that`Iso`to an actualpath of types(univalence via`Glue`)

agda
Copy code
p-reify : ∀ {Id} → Genealogy Id ≡ Σ (Serial Id) WF
p-reify  isoToPath reifyIso

isoToPathis defined usingGlueandisoToEquiv.agda.github.io

Step A3 — From thatpath, recover an`Iso`whose forward map is transport

agda
Copy code
transportIso : ∀ {Id} → Iso (Genealogy Id) (Σ (Serial Id) WF)
transportIso  pathToIso p-reify

And you can connect it back to your “handwritten”reify:

agda
Copy code
reify≡transport : ∀ {Id} (g : Genealogy Id)
  → transport p-reify g ≡ reify g
reify≡transport g   sym (transportIsoToPath reifyIso g)

Becausetransport (isoToPath f)agrees withf.fun.agda.github.io

Why this matters in practice

Nowany theoremyou proved aboutΣ (Serial Id) WFcan be “pulled back” toGenealogy Idbytransport(or by the extractedIso).

4) Recipe B: The “round-trip correctness ⇒ equality of genealogies” workflow (your Structure→Property→Equality loop)

A very common goal: proveg₁ ≡ g₂by comparing their canonical serialized forms.

If your serialization is injective up to the invariants/canonicalization you chose, you can do:

agda
Copy code
canon : ∀ {Id} → Genealogy Id → Σ (Serial Id) WF
canon  Iso.fun reifyIso

-- If the codomain is a set (often true for concrete serial formats),
-- you can turn equality of serializations into equality of genealogies:
genealogy-ext :
  ∀ {Id} (g₁ g₂ : Genealogy Id)
  → canon g₁ ≡ canon g₂
  → g₁ ≡ g₂
genealogy-ext g₁ g₂ eq   isoFunInjective reifyIso g₁ g₂ eq

isoFunInjectiveis inCubical.Foundations.Isomorphismagda.github.ioand is exactly the “reify is injective because it has an inverse” principle.

Practical genealogy meaning

  • Yourcanonicalreification (sorted edges, normalized spouse symmetry, canonical ID renaming, etc.) becomes the equality decision boundary.
  • “Equality of structures” becomes “equality of reified normal forms” (which is often computational / decidable).

5) Recipe C:Alpha-equivalence / person-renamingas a path, then`pathToIso`gives the renaming program for free

This one is gold for “IDs as implementation detail”.

Suppose you have an equivalence between ID types:

agda
Copy code
eId : Id₀ ≃ Id₁
pId : Id₀ ≡ Id₁
pId  ua eId

uais the inverse part of the univalence isomorphism betweenA ≡ BandA ≃ B.agda.github.io

Now lift that equality through your structure functor:

agda
Copy code
pG : Genealogy Id₀ ≡ Genealogy Id₁
pG  cong Genealogy pId

Finally extract the concrete renaming functions:

agda
Copy code
renameGenealogy : Genealogy Id₀ → Genealogy Id₁
renameGenealogy  Iso.fun (pathToIso pG)

unrenameGenealogy : Genealogy Id₁ → Genealogy Id₀
unrenameGenealogy  Iso.inv (pathToIso pG)

Practical genealogy meaning

This is exactly what you want when:

  • you serialize withFin nbut later reindex people
  • you canonicalize by “renaming IDs into first-appearance order”
  • you compare genealogies “up to relabeling”

You can do the whole relabelingas transport, butpathToIsomakes it a clean programmatic API.

6) Recipe D: “Decomposition of a structure” (record ↔ Σ/× normal form), then reuse generic theorems on Σ-types

This matches your “decomposition of a complete genealogy tree via reification”.

Often you want acanonical decomposition typefor:

  • generic recursion/induction
  • generic serialization/printing
  • generic equality checking
  • generic “merge” or “diff” algorithms

Example: treatGenealogy Idas a Σ of its fields:

agda
Copy code
GenealogyΣ : (Id : Type) → Type
GenealogyΣ Id  Σ[ info ∈ (Id → Type) ] (Rel → Id → Id → Type)

toΣ : ∀ {Id} → Genealogy Id → GenealogyΣ Id
toΣ g  info g , edge g

fromΣ : ∀ {Id} → GenealogyΣ Id → Genealogy Id
fromΣ (i , e) .info  i
fromΣ (i , e) .edge  e

toΣ-fromΣ : ∀ {Id} (x : GenealogyΣ Id) → toΣ (fromΣ x) ≡ x
toΣ-fromΣ _  refl

fromΣ-toΣ : ∀ {Id} (g : Genealogy Id) → fromΣ (toΣ g) ≡ g
fromΣ-toΣ _  refl

decompIso : ∀ {Id} → Iso (Genealogy Id) (GenealogyΣ Id)
decompIso  record
  { fun  toΣ ; inv  fromΣ ; sec  toΣ-fromΣ ; ret  fromΣ-toΣ }

Now you can turn this into atype equalityand back:

agda
Copy code
p-decomp : ∀ {Id} → Genealogy Id ≡ GenealogyΣ Id
p-decomp  isoToPath decompIso

decompIso' : ∀ {Id} → Iso (Genealogy Id) (GenealogyΣ Id)
decompIso'  pathToIso p-decomp

So you can:

  • write “serialization” against the Σ-normal form
  • then transport the result back to the record form

(Again,isoToPathisGlue-based.agda.github.ioandpathToIsois transport-based.leibniz-stt.github.io)

7) Recipe E: Transportingproperties(invariants) across your structure/serialization equality using`substIso`

When you have apath of typesp : A ≡ B, you frequently need not justA → B, but also

  • P : A → Typetransported toB → Type
  • “invariant types” transported along a path

Cubical.Foundations.Transportgives:

agda
Copy code
substIso : ∀ {ℓ ℓ'} {A : Type ℓ} (B : A → Type ℓ') {x y : A}
        → (p : x ≡ y) → Iso (B x) (B y)
substIso B p  pathToIso (cong B p)

leibniz-stt.github.io

How this shows up in genealogy work

Say you have an invariant living over thecarrier type(IDs):

agda
Copy code
NoSelfSpouse : ∀ {Id} → Genealogy Id → Type
NoSelfSpouse g  (x : Id) → edge g spouse x x → ⊥

If you rename IDs viapId : Id₀ ≡ Id₁, thenpG : Genealogy Id₀ ≡ Genealogy Id₁as above, you can move invariants across:

agda
Copy code
NoSelfSpouse-renamed :
  (g : Genealogy Id₀)
  → NoSelfSpouse g
  → NoSelfSpouse (transport pG g)
NoSelfSpouse-renamed g inv   transport (λ i → NoSelfSpouse (transport pG g)) inv

In bigger developments,substIso(andsubstEquiv) are often nicer because they give you explicit inverses and round-trip lemmas immediately.

8) Recipe F: Proving the loop really closes —`pathToIso (isoToPath f)`is your original`Iso`(for sets)

This is the formal “Structure↔Equality↔Structure” closure.

If your types are sets (true for most concrete serialized formats), you can prove:

agda
Copy code
pathToIso-isoToPath :
  ∀ {ℓ} {A B : Type ℓ}
  → (hA : isSet A) → (hB : isSet B)
  → (f : Iso A B)
  → pathToIso (isoToPath f) ≡ f
pathToIso-isoToPath hA hB f   Iso≡Set hA hB
    (pathToIso (isoToPath f)) f
    (λ x → transportIsoToPath f x)
    (λ y → transportIsoToPath⁻ f y)
  • transportIsoToPath/transportIsoToPath⁻are in the libraryagda.github.io
  • Iso≡Setis the “two isos are equal if their components are pointwise equal” lemma for set-level carriersagda.github.io
  • pathToIsodefinition is transport/transport⁻leibniz-stt.github.io
  • isoToPathis Glue-basedagda.github.io

Why this matters

It means you can freely move between:

  • “I have anIso(program + laws)”
  • “I have a type equalityA ≡ B(rewrite/transport engine)”

…and you don’t lose computational content.

9) How to apply these patterns to “Free Tree genealogy reification” specifically

A good architecture for your domain is:

  1. Syntax / Free layer A “free genealogy program” describing edits/relations (add person, add marriage, etc.). You normalize it (sort edges, canonicalize IDs, merge duplicates, etc.).
  2. Semantic layer A structuredGenealogy Idwith invariants.
  3. Serialization layer A concreteSerial(lists/vectors/JSON AST) +WF.

Then you prove anIsobetween semantic and canonical serialized layers:

  • Iso (Genealogy Id) (Σ SerialCanon WF)
  • turn it into a path:isoToPath
  • usepathToIsoto extract coercionsas transport programswhen you want to rewrite types generically (esp. dependent proofs)

The key trick is:make your serialization canonical(or define a canonical quotient) so that “reify equality” corresponds to “structure equality”.

Alternative approaches (often useful alongside`pathToIso`)

  1. UsepathToEquiv+ univalence directly Sometimes you wantEquivinstead ofIso(especially for higher structure, or to compose equivalences). The univalence module exposes that(A ≡ B) ≃ (A ≃ B)is itself an equivalence (viapathToEquivandua).agda.github.io Then:
    • p : A ≡ B→pathToEquiv p : A ≃ B
    • e : A ≃ B→ua e : A ≡ B
  2. Use the Structure Identity Principle (SIP) For genealogies-as-structures-on-a-carrier, SIP can let you avoid hand-buildingisoToPatharguments: you show your structure notion is “univalent”, then equality of structured objects corresponds to structure-preserving equivalences. (The cubical library hasCubical.Foundations.SIP.)leibniz-stt.github.io

Related GitHub repositories / codebases to mine for patterns

  • agda/cubical— the Cubical standard library wherepathToIso,isoToPath,ua,SIPlive.GitHub
  • agda/agda— the compiler + Cubical mode implementation and docs.agda.readthedocs.io
  • cqts/introduction-to-cubical(course notes site repo) — lots of small, copyable “transport/univalence” patterns (great for building intuition and small lemmas you’ll reuse).cqts.github.io

If you want, paste atinyfragment of your current genealogy types (even just yourPersonId, your edge representation, and your current serialization target), and I’ll adapt Recipe A/B into a compiling, domain-faithfulIso+isoToPath+pathToIsoloop that matches your exact “free tree → canonical serial → semantic graph” pipeline.

What`pathToIso`is doing for you in the genealogy setting

In Cubical Agda,pathToIsois the “computational back-edge” of the loop:

  • You prove aPath(a type equality) because your domain argument says two representations are “the same thing”.
  • You then wantactual functionsto convert values back and forth.
  • pathToIsoturns that type equality into anIsoso you can extractfun/invconversion functions and use them in programs.

The two glue pieces that make this practical (and not just philosophical):

  • transportIsoToPath : (f : Iso A B) (x : A) → transport (isoToPath f) x ≡ f .fun xlets you replace “mysterious transport” with your actual conversion function.agda-libraries
  • Forset-level types, there’s a tight relationship betweenIso A BandA ≡ B(soisoToPathandpathToIsobehave like inverses up to the right notion).agda-libraries

That’s the core loop you want for “free tree → canonical serial → semantic graph”:

Structure(representation) → prove aPropertyabout it → turn equivalence intoEquality→transportthe property → recover a newStructureconversion bypathToIso.

GEDCOM concepts we’ll explicitly model (subset)

The dummy examples and the Agda recipes below are grounded in these GEDCOM v7 ideas:

  • A GEDCOM dataset is a header, followed by 0+ records, ending with a trailer.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • Pointers are either a real cross-reference pointer or the special@VOID@placeholder (“void pointer”).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
    • @VOID@is used when data is removed but structure/pointers must remain (export/round-trip friendliness).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
    • @VOID@can also appear as a placeholder for an unknown child in aFAMrecord.
  • The core linkage is betweenINDI(individual) andFAM(family) records, and linkages are expected to be consistent in both directions:
    • FAMusesHUSB,WIFE,CHILpointers intoINDI.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
    • The pointed-toINDIrecords should point back usingFAMS(as spouse) andFAMC(as child).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • Pedigree / child-family status usesPEDI(enum:ADOPTED,BIRTH,FOSTER,SEALING,OTHERwith aPHRASE).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • Adoption can be described withADOP+FAMCand anADOPenum that says which spouse adopted (HUSB,WIFE,BOTH).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • Association (laterals, e.g. godparents) usesASSOwithROLE(e.g.GODP).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • Documented extension tags are tied to meaning by aURIinHEAD.SCHMA.TAG, and semantic equivalence is by URI, not by the literal tag name.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • GEDCOM explicitly allows alternative modeling choices (e.g., separation/reunion can be one family with multiple events vs multiple families with the same spouses).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

These are exactly the “domain states of affairs” you can turn into Paths, and then intoIsoviapathToIso.

Extensive dummy GEDCOM fragments

Example 1 — Step-family + laterals + void-child placeholder + documented extensions + marriage age

This is a single GEDCOM dataset (header + records + trailer). The constructs used are consistent with:

  • dataset structureThe FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • FAMandINDIlinkageThe FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • backlink expectationThe FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • @VOID@placeholder child
  • association roleGODPThe FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • marriage event with wife age substructureThe FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • documented extension tags mapped viaSCHMA.TAGto URIsThe FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • FAMC+PEDIfor birth/other pedigree linksThe FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
gedcom
Copy code
0 HEAD
1 GEDC
2 VERS 7.0
2 FORM LINEAGE-LINKED
1 SCHMA
2 TAG _SKYPEID http://example.org/uri/contact/skype
2 TAG _CID     http://example.org/uri/contact/customer-id

0 @I1@ INDI
1 NAME Joao /Silva/
1 SEX M
1 BIRT
2 DATE 14 FEB 1980
2 PLAC Sao Paulo, Brazil
1 FAMS @F1@
1 _SKYPEID joao.silva

0 @I2@ INDI
1 NAME Maria /Souza/
1 SEX F
1 FAMS @F1@
1 FAMS @F2@
1 _CID CUST-00991

0 @I3@ INDI
1 NAME Ana /Silva/
1 SEX F
1 BIRT
2 DATE 20 APR 2005
2 PLAC Sao Paulo, Brazil
2 FAMC @F1@
1 FAMC @F1@
2 PEDI BIRTH
1 FAMC @F2@
2 PEDI OTHER
3 PHRASE Step family (moved 2010)

1 ASSO @I4@
2 ROLE GODP

0 @I4@ INDI
1 NAME Carlos /Pereira/
1 SEX M
1 FAMS @F2@

0 @F1@ FAM
1 HUSB @I1@
1 WIFE @I2@
1 MARR
2 DATE 01 JAN 2000
2 PLAC Sao Paulo, Brazil
2 WIFE
3 AGE 22y
1 CHIL @I3@

0 @F2@ FAM
1 HUSB @I2@
1 WIFE @I4@
1 MARR
2 DATE 01 JUN 2010
1 CHIL @I3@
1 CHIL @VOID@
2 PHRASE Unknown infant

0 TRLR

Why this example isusefulfor`pathToIso`

It contains two distinct “canonicalization” pressures:

  1. Schema normalization:_SKYPEID/_CIDare meaningful by URI, not by tag spelling.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  2. Graph normalization: family links are a graph with intended bidirectional consistency.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

Both of these become “proof obligations” you can use to build Paths between types of representations.

Example 2 — Adoption modeled with`ADOP`+`FAMC`+ “who adopted” enum

Grounded in the adoption structure andADOPenum semantics.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

gedcom
Copy code
0 HEAD
1 GEDC
2 VERS 7.0
2 FORM LINEAGE-LINKED

0 @I10@ INDI
1 NAME Beatriz /Lima/
1 SEX F

0 @I11@ INDI
1 NAME Rafael /Lima/
1 SEX M

0 @I12@ INDI
1 NAME Clara /Lima/
1 SEX F
1 FAMC @F10@
2 PEDI ADOPTED
1 ADOP
2 DATE 12 DEC 2012
2 FAMC @F10@
3 ADOP WIFE

0 @F10@ FAM
1 HUSB @I11@
1 WIFE @I10@
1 CHIL @I12@
1 MARR
2 DATE 10 OCT 2000

0 TRLR

Interpretation pressure:

  • You may want a canonical semantic representation where “adopted-by-wife” becomes a normalized edge or an event attached to the child or family.
  • There are multiple plausible internal models; proving they’re equivalent is exactly wherepathToIsobecomes a workhorse.

Example 3 — Two different extension-tag spellings, same URI semantics

GEDCOM’s spec states that the meaning of a documented extension tag is identified by URI, so different tag spellings can be semantically equivalent if they map to the same URI in the schema.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

Doc A:

gedcom
Copy code
0 HEAD
1 SCHMA
2 TAG _SKYPEID http://example.org/uri/contact/skype

0 @I1@ INDI
1 NAME Joao /Silva/
1 _SKYPEID joao.silva
0 TRLR

Doc B (renamed tag):

gedcom
Copy code
0 HEAD
1 SCHMA
2 TAG _SKP http://example.org/uri/contact/skype

0 @I1@ INDI
1 NAME Joao /Silva/
1 _SKP joao.silva
0 TRLR

This is a perfect “state of affairs → Path” story:

  • State of affairs:_SKYPEIDand_SKPare different surface tags, but schema says both mean the same URI.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • Canonical serial: normalize extensions to URI keys.
  • Equality: “these two extension-tag universes are the same up to schema equivalence”.
  • Extract Iso: usepathToIsoto get actual renaming functions you can use in your reifier/serializer.

Example 4 — Redaction/removal preserved structurally via`@VOID@`

GEDCOM v7 explicitly notes that pointers to removed structures should be replaced with@VOID@, and structures may remain but with empty payloads so exports can round-trip.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

gedcom
Copy code
0 @F20@ FAM
1 HUSB @VOID@
1 WIFE @I2@
1 CHIL @I3@
1 MARR
2 DATE
2 PLAC

The “domain pressure” here is: your semantic graph probably wantsexplicit unknowns(not “missing fields”), and your canonical serial wants a stable placeholder you can round-trip.@VOID@pushes you toward a dedicated pointer type in Agda (Ptr A void | ref a), and you’ll wantIsobetween multiple internal encodings of “optional reference”.

Agda modeling aligned to your pipeline

We’ll set up three layers:

  1. Free tree: close to GEDCOM record structure; local extension tags; XREF-like IDs.
  2. Canonical serial: same information but normalized:
    • extension keys normalized to URIs (schema meaning)The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
    • IDs reindexed to a canonical finite index type (e.g.Fin n)
  3. Semantic graph: your “meaningful” relation-level view (parents/spouses/associations), derived from records and free of spelling/ordering artifacts.

Below is a compact, domain-faithful “core” that is enough to demonstrateIso ↔ isoToPath ↔ pathToIsoon real GEDCOM-like content.

Core Cubical tools we’ll rely on

From the Cubical library (as listed in youragda-libraries.txt):

  • Iso,isoToPath,pathToIso,compIso,invIso, plus Π-type iso helpers likecodomainIsoDep.agda-libraries
  • transportand friends (includingtransport⁻).agda-libraries
  • The crucial bridge lemmatransportIsoToPath.agda-libraries

Concrete Cubical Agda snippets

0) Minimal GEDCOM-faithful core types

agda
Copy code
{-# OPTIONS --cubical #-}

module Genealogy.GEDCOM.PathToIso where

open import Cubical.Foundations.Prelude
open import Cubical.Foundations.Isomorphism
open import Cubical.Foundations.Transport
open import Cubical.Data.Sigma
open import Cubical.Data.List as List using (List; []; _∷_; map)
open import Cubical.Data.Maybe as Maybe using (Maybe; nothing; just)
open import Cubical.Data.Unit using (Unit; tt)
open import Cubical.Data.Empty as ⊥ using (⊥)

open Iso  -- lets us write `fun`, `inv`, `sec`, `ret`

-- GEDCOM pointer: either a real reference or the void pointer (@VOID@).
data Ptr {ℓ : Level} (A : Type ℓ) : Type ℓ where
  void : Ptr A
  ref  : A → Ptr A

mapPtr : ∀ {ℓ ℓ'} {A : Type ℓ} {B : Type ℓ'} → (A → B) → Ptr A → Ptr B
mapPtr f void     void
mapPtr f (ref a)  ref (f a)

-- A tiny subset of GEDCOM enums we actually use in the examples:
data Sex : Type where M F U : Sex

data Pedi : Type where
  ADOPTED BIRTH FOSTER SEALING : Pedi
  OTHER : (phrase : String) → Pedi

data AdopBy : Type where HUSB WIFE BOTH : AdopBy

data Role : Type where GODP : Role  -- godparent example (ASSO/ROLE)

record Date : Type where constructor mkDate ; field txt : String
record Place : Type where constructor mkPlace ; field txt : String
record Age : Type where constructor mkAge ; field txt : String

-- Individual events: enough to show BIRT with optional FAMC, and ADOP with FAMC + who.
data IndiEvent (F : Type) : Type where
  BIRT : Maybe Date → Maybe Place → Maybe F → IndiEvent F
  ADOP : Maybe Date → F → Maybe AdopBy → IndiEvent F

data FamEvent : Type where
  MARR : Maybe Date → Maybe Place → Maybe Age → FamEvent   -- keep one age for demo
  DIV  : Maybe Date → Maybe Place → FamEvent

-- INDI record core (subset): names, sex, family links, associations, events, extensions.
record IndiRec (P F Ext : Type) : Type where
  constructor indi
  field
    names  : List String
    sex    : Maybe Sex
    famc   : List (F × Pedi)          -- INDI.FAMC with PEDI (step/adopt/etc.)
    fams   : List F                   -- INDI.FAMS
    asso   : List (P × Role)          -- ASSO @INDI@ / ROLE ...
    events : List (IndiEvent F)
    ext    : List (Ext × String)      -- extension payloads (keyed by Ext)

-- FAM record core (subset): partners, children (may include void), events, extensions.
record FamRec (P Ext : Type) : Type where
  constructor fam
  field
    husb   : Ptr P
    wife   : Ptr P
    chil   : List (Ptr P)
    events : List FamEvent
    ext    : List (Ext × String)

-- Dataset: a forest of records, keyed by IDs.
Dataset : (P F Ext : Type) → Type
Dataset P F Ext   (List (P × IndiRec P F Ext)) × (List (F × FamRec P Ext))

How this matches the spec (conceptually):

  • Ptrcorresponds to GEDCOM pointer syntax, including@VOID@.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • famcincludesPEDIincludingOTHER + PHRASE, matching thePEDIenum guidance.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • ASSOwithROLEsuch asGODPis explicitly shown in the spec examples.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • Family links (HUSB/WIFE/CHIL) and backlink expectations are captured by storing both sides; we’ll define a property to check consistency.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

1) Iso combinators you’ll use constantly

These are the “recipe ingredients” for lifting anIsoon IDs/tags into anIsoon whole datasets:

agda
Copy code
-- Iso over lists by mapping.
ListIso : ∀ {ℓ ℓ'} {A : Type ℓ} {B : Type ℓ'} → Iso A B → Iso (List A) (List B)
fun (ListIso i)  map (fun i)
inv (ListIso i)  map (inv i)
sec (ListIso i) []        refl
sec (ListIso i) (x ∷ xs)   cong (λ x' → x' ∷ map (inv i) (map (fun i) xs)) (sec i x)
  ∙ cong (λ xs' → x ∷ xs') (sec (ListIso i) xs)
ret (ListIso i) []        refl
ret (ListIso i) (y ∷ ys)   cong (λ y' → y' ∷ map (fun i) (map (inv i) ys)) (ret i y)
  ∙ cong (λ ys' → y ∷ ys') (ret (ListIso i) ys)

PtrIso : ∀ {ℓ ℓ'} {A : Type ℓ} {B : Type ℓ'} → Iso A B → Iso (Ptr A) (Ptr B)
fun (PtrIso i)  mapPtr (fun i)
inv (PtrIso i)  mapPtr (inv i)
sec (PtrIso i) void     refl
sec (PtrIso i) (ref x)  cong ref (sec i x)
ret (PtrIso i) void     refl
ret (PtrIso i) (ref y)  cong ref (ret i y)

MaybeIso : ∀ {ℓ ℓ'} {A : Type ℓ} {B : Type ℓ'} → Iso A B → Iso (Maybe A) (Maybe B)
fun (MaybeIso i) nothing   nothing
fun (MaybeIso i) (just a)  just (fun i a)
inv (MaybeIso i) nothing   nothing
inv (MaybeIso i) (just b)  just (inv i b)
sec (MaybeIso i) nothing   refl
sec (MaybeIso i) (just a)  cong just (sec i a)
ret (MaybeIso i) nothing   refl
ret (MaybeIso i) (just b)  cong just (ret i b)

-- Product Iso (pairs).
×Iso : ∀ {ℓ₁ ℓ₂ ℓ₁' ℓ₂'}
     {A : Type ℓ₁} {B : Type ℓ₂} {A' : Type ℓ₁'} {B' : Type ℓ₂'}
   → Iso A A' → Iso B B' → Iso (A × B) (A' × B')
fun (×Iso i j) (a , b)  fun i a , fun j b
inv (×Iso i j) (a' , b')  inv i a' , inv j b'
sec (×Iso i j) (a , b) k  sec i a k , sec j b k
ret (×Iso i j) (a' , b') k  ret i a' k , ret j b' k

Why these matter for your pipeline:

  • Reindexing people/families is “just” anIsoon ID types.
  • Schema normalization (local tag → URI key) is “just” anIsoon extension-key types.
  • Everything else isstructural lifting:ListIso,PtrIso,×Iso, etc.

(These combinator patterns are exactly the kind of building blocks the Cubical library itself uses for Π/Σ types and structured transport.agda-libraries)

2) Lifting`Iso`through GEDCOM record cores

agda
Copy code
mapIndiEvent : ∀ {F F'} → (F → F') → IndiEvent F → IndiEvent F'
mapIndiEvent f (BIRT d p mf)      BIRT d p (Maybe.map f mf)
mapIndiEvent f (ADOP d fam who)   ADOP d (f fam) who

IndiEventIso : ∀ {F F'} → Iso F F' → Iso (IndiEvent F) (IndiEvent F')
fun (IndiEventIso i) e  mapIndiEvent (fun i) e
inv (IndiEventIso i) e  mapIndiEvent (inv i) e
sec (IndiEventIso i) (BIRT d p mf)  cong (BIRT d p) (MaybeIso i .sec mf)
sec (IndiEventIso i) (ADOP d fam who)  cong (λ fam' → ADOP d fam' who) (sec i fam)
ret (IndiEventIso i) (BIRT d p mf)  cong (BIRT d p) (MaybeIso i .ret mf)
ret (IndiEventIso i) (ADOP d fam who)  cong (λ fam' → ADOP d fam' who) (ret i fam)

IndiRecIso : ∀ {P P' F F' Ext Ext'}
  → Iso P P' → Iso F F' → Iso Ext Ext'
  → Iso (IndiRec P F Ext) (IndiRec P' F' Ext')
fun (IndiRecIso iP iF iE) r   indi (IndiRec.names r)
       (IndiRec.sex r)
       (map (λ (f , pedi) → fun iF f , pedi) (IndiRec.famc r))
       (map (fun iF) (IndiRec.fams r))
       (map (λ (p , role) → fun iP p , role) (IndiRec.asso r))
       (map (fun (IndiEventIso iF)) (IndiRec.events r))
       (map (λ (k , v) → fun iE k , v) (IndiRec.ext r))

inv (IndiRecIso iP iF iE) r   indi (IndiRec.names r)
       (IndiRec.sex r)
       (map (λ (f , pedi) → inv iF f , pedi) (IndiRec.famc r))
       (map (inv iF) (IndiRec.fams r))
       (map (λ (p , role) → inv iP p , role) (IndiRec.asso r))
       (map (inv (IndiEventIso iF)) (IndiRec.events r))
       (map (λ (k , v) → inv iE k , v) (IndiRec.ext r))

-- Proofs are routine by record-path + list induction; omitted here for readability.
-- In real code you can prove them field-by-field using `ListIso`/`MaybeIso`/`IndiEventIso`.
postulate
  IndiRecIso-sec : ∀ {P P' F F' Ext Ext'} (iP : Iso P P') (iF : Iso F F') (iE : Iso Ext Ext')
                 → (r : IndiRec P F Ext) → inv (IndiRecIso iP iF iE) (fun (IndiRecIso iP iF iE) r) ≡ r
  IndiRecIso-ret : ∀ {P P' F F' Ext Ext'} (iP : Iso P P') (iF : Iso F F') (iE : Iso Ext Ext')
                 → (r : IndiRec P' F' Ext') → fun (IndiRecIso iP iF iE) (inv (IndiRecIso iP iF iE) r) ≡ r

sec (IndiRecIso iP iF iE)  IndiRecIso-sec iP iF iE
ret (IndiRecIso iP iF iE)  IndiRecIso-ret iP iF iE

FamRecIso : ∀ {P P' Ext Ext'} → Iso P P' → Iso Ext Ext' → Iso (FamRec P Ext) (FamRec P' Ext')
fun (FamRecIso iP iE) r   fam (fun (PtrIso iP) (FamRec.husb r))
      (fun (PtrIso iP) (FamRec.wife r))
      (fun (ListIso (PtrIso iP)) (FamRec.chil r))
      (FamRec.events r)
      (map (λ (k , v) → fun iE k , v) (FamRec.ext r))

inv (FamRecIso iP iE) r   fam (inv (PtrIso iP) (FamRec.husb r))
      (inv (PtrIso iP) (FamRec.wife r))
      (inv (ListIso (PtrIso iP)) (FamRec.chil r))
      (FamRec.events r)
      (map (λ (k , v) → inv iE k , v) (FamRec.ext r))

postulate
  FamRecIso-sec : ∀ {P P' Ext Ext'} (iP : Iso P P') (iE : Iso Ext Ext')
                → (r : FamRec P Ext) → inv (FamRecIso iP iE) (fun (FamRecIso iP iE) r) ≡ r
  FamRecIso-ret : ∀ {P P' Ext Ext'} (iP : Iso P P') (iE : Iso Ext Ext')
                → (r : FamRec P' Ext') → fun (FamRecIso iP iE) (inv (FamRecIso iP iE) r) ≡ r

sec (FamRecIso iP iE)  FamRecIso-sec iP iE
ret (FamRecIso iP iE)  FamRecIso-ret iP iE

Why I allowedpostulatehere: The proofs are straightforward but verbose (list induction + record extensionality). In “real code”, you either (a) prove these field-by-field usingListIsoandMaybeIso, or (b) encode the records as nested Σ/products and letΣ-cong-iso/×Isocompose proofs mechanically.agda-libraries

If you want, you can replace these postulates by explicit proofs once you settle your final record shapes; the recipe pattern won’t change.

3) The dataset Iso (the heart of reindexing + schema normalization)

agda
Copy code
DatasetIso : ∀ {P P' F F' Ext Ext'}
  → Iso P P' → Iso F F' → Iso Ext Ext'
  → Iso (Dataset P F Ext) (Dataset P' F' Ext')
DatasetIso iP iF iE   ×Iso
    (ListIso (×Iso iP (IndiRecIso iP iF iE)))
    (ListIso (×Iso iF (FamRecIso iP iE)))

This is the practical “lift” you will use everywhere:

  • If you can buildIsobetweenID types(XREF renaming/reindexing), andIsobetweenextension key types(tag spelling ↔ URI), you immediately get anIsobetween whole genealogies.

Now: the actual`Iso → Path → Iso`loop on a GEDCOM-grounded example

Example IDs and extension schemas

We’ll model Example 1 with finite IDs (think@I1@,@F1@etc):

agda
Copy code
data IRef : Type where I1 I2 I3 I4 : IRef
data FRef : Type where F1 F2 : FRef

-- Two different *surface* extension-tag alphabets:
data ExtA : Type where SKYPEID CID : ExtA    -- corresponds to _SKYPEID, _CID
data ExtB : Type where SKP     CID' : ExtB   -- corresponds to _SKP, _CID

-- Canonical extension keys (URIs), treated as a small finite universe here.
data ExtURI : Type where skypeURI customerIdURI : ExtURI

schemaA : Iso ExtA ExtURI
fun schemaA SKYPEID  skypeURI
fun schemaA CID      customerIdURI
inv schemaA skypeURI         SKYPEID
inv schemaA customerIdURI    CID
sec schemaA SKYPEID  refl
sec schemaA CID      refl
ret schemaA skypeURI         refl
ret schemaA customerIdURI    refl

schemaB : Iso ExtB ExtURI
fun schemaB SKP    skypeURI
fun schemaB CID'   customerIdURI
inv schemaB skypeURI         SKP
inv schemaB customerIdURI    CID'
sec schemaB SKP    refl
sec schemaB CID'   refl
ret schemaB skypeURI         refl
ret schemaB customerIdURI    refl

This is exactly the “documented extension tags meaning-by-URI” mechanism in the spec.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

The example dataset (core reification of Example 1)

agda
Copy code
open IndiRec
open FamRec
open Date
open Place
open Age

docA : Dataset IRef FRef ExtA
docA   ( (I1 , indi ("Joao /Silva/" ∷ [])
              (just M)
              []
              (F1 ∷ [])
              []
              (BIRT (just (mkDate "14 FEB 1980"))
                    (just (mkPlace "Sao Paulo, Brazil"))
                    nothing ∷ [])
              ((SKYPEID , "joao.silva") ∷ [])
    )
    ∷ (I2 , indi ("Maria /Souza/" ∷ [])
              (just F)
              []
              (F1 ∷ F2 ∷ [])
              []
              []
              ((CID , "CUST-00991") ∷ [])
    )
    ∷ (I3 , indi ("Ana /Silva/" ∷ [])
              (just F)
              ((F1 , BIRTH) ∷ (F2 , OTHER "Step family (moved 2010)") ∷ [])
              []
              ((I4 , GODP) ∷ [])
              (BIRT (just (mkDate "20 APR 2005"))
                    (just (mkPlace "Sao Paulo, Brazil"))
                    (just F1) ∷ [])
              []
    )
    ∷ (I4 , indi ("Carlos /Pereira/" ∷ [])
              (just M)
              []
              (F2 ∷ [])
              []
              []
              []
    )
    ∷ []
  )
  ,
  ( (F1 , fam (ref I1) (ref I2)
              (ref I3 ∷ [])
              (MARR (just (mkDate "01 JAN 2000"))
                    (just (mkPlace "Sao Paulo, Brazil"))
                    (just (mkAge "22y")) ∷ [])
              []
    )
    ∷ (F2 , fam (ref I2) (ref I4)
              (ref I3 ∷ void ∷ [])
              (MARR (just (mkDate "01 JUN 2010")) nothing nothing ∷ [])
              []
    )
    ∷ []
  )

This matches the spirit of:

  • FAMstructure withHUSB/WIFE/CHILpointers and@VOID@placeholder child.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • FAMC+PEDIencoding birth vs “other (step)” with a phrase.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • ASSO+ROLE GODP.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • MARRwith wife age substructure (we modeled only one age field).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

Canonicalization step 1: normalize extension tags to URIs

Build theIsobetween the dataset that uses surface tags (ExtA) and the dataset that uses URI keys (ExtURI):

agda
Copy code
-- ID isos are identity here (we are only normalizing extension keys).
idIso : ∀ {ℓ} {A : Type ℓ} → Iso A A
fun idIso x  x
inv idIso x  x
sec idIso x  refl
ret idIso x  refl

docA→URIIso : Iso (Dataset IRef FRef ExtA) (Dataset IRef FRef ExtURI)
docA→URIIso  DatasetIso idIso idIso schemaA

Now thePathbetween those two dataset types is:

agda
Copy code
docA→URIPath : Dataset IRef FRef ExtA ≡ Dataset IRef FRef ExtURI
docA→URIPath  isoToPath docA→URIIso

And now the key move:

agda
Copy code
docA→URIIso' : Iso (Dataset IRef FRef ExtA) (Dataset IRef FRef ExtURI)
docA→URIIso'  pathToIso docA→URIPath

This is a fullIso → Path → Isoloop.

In practice, this is how youcomplete the Structure↔Equality loop:

  • UseisoToPathonce to get a definitional bridge for transport/rewriting.
  • Later, if you only have the equality (maybe from SIP or from a larger equivalence proof), recover functions withpathToIso.

Transport computation: get rid of mysterious transports

If you want to transport avalueacross the equality, transport gives you a value in the canonical type.

But you’ll want it to compute to your conversion function. That’s where the lemma matters:agda-libraries

agda
Copy code
docA-normalized : Dataset IRef FRef ExtURI
docA-normalized  transport docA→URIPath docA

docA-normalized≡fun :
  transport docA→URIPath docA ≡ fun docA→URIIso docA
docA-normalized≡fun   transportIsoToPath docA→URIIso docA

This is the key “practicality hook”: you can do formal reasoning viatransport, but still compute/execute thefunpart of yourIso.

Recipe A–F: Domain “state of affairs” → Path →`pathToIso`→ usable conversions

Below, each recipe is written as arepeatable patternyou can plug into your actual free-tree GEDCOM reification pipeline.

Recipe A — Optional pointers vs`@VOID@`pointers

State of affairs (GEDCOM grounded)

GEDCOM distinguishes “no pointer” (optional substructure absent) from “pointer present but redacted/unknown” using@VOID@.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

In code, you’ll often havetwo internal designs:

  • Design 1:Maybe Pfor spouse/child refs
  • Design 2:Ptr Pwithvoid | ref p

Build the Path and extract the Iso

agda
Copy code
Maybe≃Ptr : ∀ {ℓ} {A : Type ℓ} → Iso (Maybe A) (Ptr A)
fun Maybe≃Ptr nothing   void
fun Maybe≃Ptr (just a)  ref a
inv Maybe≃Ptr void      nothing
inv Maybe≃Ptr (ref a)   just a
sec Maybe≃Ptr nothing   refl
sec Maybe≃Ptr (just a)  refl
ret Maybe≃Ptr void      refl
ret Maybe≃Ptr (ref a)   refl

Maybe≡Ptr : ∀ {ℓ} {A : Type ℓ} → Maybe A ≡ Ptr A
Maybe≡Ptr  isoToPath Maybe≃Ptr

Maybe≃Ptr' : ∀ {ℓ} {A : Type ℓ} → Iso (Maybe A) (Ptr A)
Maybe≃Ptr'  pathToIso Maybe≡Ptr

Practical GEDCOM use

  • When redacting a spouse ID, you canpreserve structureby turningjust pintovoidrather than dropping the field, matching GEDCOM’s intent for round-tripping redactions.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • ForFAM.CHIL,voidis a first-class placeholder for “unknown child” (which GEDCOM explicitly illustrates).

Recipe B — Documented extension tags: rename surface tags by URI semantics

State of affairs (GEDCOM grounded)

Meaning of a documented extension tag is identified by URI. Two documents can use different tag spellings but be semantically equivalent if schema maps them to the same URI.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

Build the Path at the “extension key universe” level

We already builtschemaA : Iso ExtA ExtURIandschemaB : Iso ExtB ExtURI.

So we get anIsobetweenExtAandExtB“through meaning”:

agda
Copy code
ExtA≃ExtB : Iso ExtA ExtB
ExtA≃ExtB  compIso schemaA (invIso schemaB)

Now lift it to whole datasets (IDs fixed):

agda
Copy code
DocA≃DocB : Iso (Dataset IRef FRef ExtA) (Dataset IRef FRef ExtB)
DocA≃DocB  DatasetIso idIso idIso ExtA≃ExtB

DocA≡DocB : Dataset IRef FRef ExtA ≡ Dataset IRef FRef ExtB
DocA≡DocB  isoToPath DocA≃DocB

DocA≃DocB-fromPath : Iso (Dataset IRef FRef ExtA) (Dataset IRef FRef ExtB)
DocA≃DocB-fromPath  pathToIso DocA≡DocB

Practical GEDCOM use

This isexactly“free tree → canonical serial” for extensions:

  • Free tree: keep raw extension tags (_SKYPEID,_CID, …) plus schema.
  • Canonical serial: normalize extensions by URI key (so two files with different tag spellings merge cleanly).

This is how you avoid “schema spelling noise” preventing equality.

Recipe C — XREF reindexing: “string IDs” → finite indices (canonical)

State of affairs (domain + GEDCOM)

GEDCOM uses cross-reference IDs (XREFs) for records; canonical serialization often wants a compact finite index space (likeFin n), which you mentioned in the pattern.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

In practice, you pick an ordering ofINDI/FAMrecords and produce anIsobetween:

  • surface ID type (e.g., parsed XREFs)
  • canonical index type

The key pattern

GivenIsoon person IDs and family IDs, you lift them to anIsoon the entire dataset usingDatasetIso.

For the demo, use a toy index type (you can replace withFin nlater):

agda
Copy code
data PIx : Type where p0 p1 p2 p3 : PIx
data FIx : Type where f0 f1 : FIx

IRef≃PIx : Iso IRef PIx
fun IRef≃PIx I1  p0
fun IRef≃PIx I2  p1
fun IRef≃PIx I3  p2
fun IRef≃PIx I4  p3
inv IRef≃PIx p0  I1
inv IRef≃PIx p1  I2
inv IRef≃PIx p2  I3
inv IRef≃PIx p3  I4
sec IRef≃PIx I1  refl
sec IRef≃PIx I2  refl
sec IRef≃PIx I3  refl
sec IRef≃PIx I4  refl
ret IRef≃PIx p0  refl
ret IRef≃PIx p1  refl
ret IRef≃PIx p2  refl
ret IRef≃PIx p3  refl

FRef≃FIx : Iso FRef FIx
fun FRef≃FIx F1  f0
fun FRef≃FIx F2  f1
inv FRef≃FIx f0  F1
inv FRef≃FIx f1  F2
sec FRef≃FIx F1  refl
sec FRef≃FIx F2  refl
ret FRef≃FIx f0  refl
ret FRef≃FIx f1  refl

Free≃Canon : Iso (Dataset IRef FRef ExtURI) (Dataset PIx FIx ExtURI)
Free≃Canon  DatasetIso IRef≃PIx FRef≃FIx idIso

Free≡Canon : Dataset IRef FRef ExtURI ≡ Dataset PIx FIx ExtURI
Free≡Canon  isoToPath Free≃Canon

Canon≃Free : Iso (Dataset PIx FIx ExtURI) (Dataset IRef FRef ExtURI)
Canon≃Free  invIso (pathToIso Free≡Canon)

Practical GEDCOM use

This is your “reification” step:

  • The free tree uses whatever ID space you get from parsing (@I1@, GUIDs, etc).
  • Canonical serial uses tight indexing (Fin n/arrays/vectors) so serialization and algorithms are stable and total.

Because this is anIso, you can:

  • transport proofs about the semantics across the reindexing,
  • and still run conversions efficiently.

Recipe D — The Structure→Property→Equality→Structure loop for GEDCOM “well-formedness”

State of affairs (GEDCOM grounded)

The spec expects that links are consistent:

  • FAM.HUSB/WIFEpointers should be matched byINDI.FAMSbacklinks.
  • FAM.CHILpointers should be matched byINDI.FAMCbacklinks.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

Define apropertyWF(well-formedness) on one representation, then transport it to another using the type equality obtained viaisoToPath.

Here’s a lightweight sketch (membership proofs omitted for brevity; in real code you’d define_∈_and lookup predicates):

agda
Copy code
WF : ∀ {P F Ext} → Dataset P F Ext → Type
WF d  Unit  -- replace with backlink consistency constraints per GEDCOM

-- Transport WF across a representation equality (the key move):
WF-transport :
  ∀ {A B : Type} (i : Iso A B) (a : A)
  → WF a → WF (transport (isoToPath i) a)
WF-transport i a w  transport (cong WF (isoToPath i)) w

Now connect the transported value to the concrete conversion function usingtransportIsoToPath:

agda
Copy code
WF-on-fun :
  ∀ {A B : Type} (i : Iso A B) (a : A)
  → WF a → WF (fun i a)
WF-on-fun i a w   subst WF (transportIsoToPath i a) (WF-transport i a w)

This is themechanicalcompletion of the loop:

  • Structure conversion:i : Iso A B
  • Equality:isoToPath i : A ≡ B
  • Transport the property:transport (cong WF (isoToPath i))
  • Return to computable structure: rewrite viatransportIsoToPathso you end up withWF (fun i a).

The bridging lemma is from the Cubical library.agda-libraries

Recipe E — Alternative GEDCOM modeling choices become isomorphic canonical models

State of affairs (GEDCOM grounded)

GEDCOM notes that a couple separating and reuniting can be represented either:

  • as multiple events in oneFAM, or
  • as multipleFAMrecords with the same spouses, each with one marriage event.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

This is a classic “many surface forms → one canonical semantic graph” situation.

How to use`pathToIso`

Define two surface types:

  • OneFammodel: one family record withevents : List FamEvent
  • ManyFammodel: list of family records each with oneMARRevent

Then:

  1. define a canonical semantic typeEpisodes(list of marriage episodes),
  2. showIso OneFam Episodes,
  3. showIso ManyFam Episodes,
  4. compose to getIso OneFam ManyFam,
  5. turn it into equality viaisoToPath,
  6. and usepathToIsowhenever you only have the equality later (e.g., after rewriting).

Sketch:

agda
Copy code
record OneFam (P : Type) : Type where
  field partners : Ptr P × Ptr P
        events   : List FamEvent

record ManyFam (P : Type) : Type where
  field partners : Ptr P × Ptr P
        episodes : List (Maybe Date × Maybe Place)  -- a canonical “marriage episodes” view

-- You build Iso OneFam ↔ ManyFam by normalizing/denormalizing events ↔ episodes.
postulate
  OneFam≃ManyFam : ∀ {P} → Iso (OneFam P) (ManyFam P)

OneFam≡ManyFam : ∀ {P} → OneFam P ≡ ManyFam P
OneFam≡ManyFam  isoToPath OneFam≃ManyFam

OneFam≃ManyFam' : ∀ {P} → Iso (OneFam P) (ManyFam P)
OneFam≃ManyFam'  pathToIso OneFam≡ManyFam

Practical use

This is how you:

  • accept multiple GEDCOM authoring styles on input,
  • but keep one canonical internal representation,
  • while preserving a proof-relevant, reversible bridge.

Recipe F — Adoption normalization (PEDI vs ADOP-event)

State of affairs (GEDCOM grounded)

  • FAMC.PEDIcan sayADOPTED.The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…
  • ADOPevent +FAMC+ADOPenum can say who adopted (WIFE,HUSB,BOTH).The FamilySearch GEDCOM Specifi…

These are different “views” that you may want to normalize into a single semantic edge/event.

How`pathToIso`fits

You define two internal representations:

  • AdoptionA: store adoption asFAMCentry withPEDI ADOPTEDplus maybe aPHRASE.
  • AdoptionB: store adoption as anADOPevent withFAMCandAdopBy.

Then you define a canonical semantic representation, say:

agda
Copy code
record AdoptionSem (F : Type) : Type where
  field fam : F
        by  : Maybe AdopBy   -- unknown vs wife/husb/both

Prove:

  • Iso AdoptionA AdoptionSem
  • Iso AdoptionB AdoptionSem
  • compose toIso AdoptionA AdoptionB
  • and then you can useisoToPath/pathToIso.

This is particularly valuable when your “free tree” representation preserves verbatim GEDCOM structure, but your “semantic graph” wants uniform edges.

Putting the whole pipeline together (your exact loop)

You can now assemble your intended pipeline:

  1. Free tree (GEDCOM-ish) Dataset XRefINDI XRefFAM ExtTagName (close to parsed GEDCOM record forest; includes schema tag spellings)
  2. Canonical serial Dataset (Fin nI) (Fin nF) URIKey (IDs reindexed; extensions normalized to URIs; stable for serialization)
  3. Semantic graph A structure derived from the canonical dataset:
    • spouse/partner edges
    • parent-child edges
    • adoption/step pedigree semantics
    • association edges (e.g., godparent)

Every arrow above can be made anIsoif you restrict to well-formed/canonical subsetsand keep enough information for reification (e.g. keep schema mappings and index assignments explicitly).

Then you get the complete “univalence/transport loop”:

  • Structure ↔ Structure:i : Iso Free Canon
  • Equality:p : Free ≡ Canon isoToPath i
  • Property transport: ifQ : Canon → Type, thentransport (cong Q p)moves proofs/data across
  • Recover conversions from equality:pathToIso p : Iso Free Canon

The “computational guarantee” that transport aligns with your functions is provided bytransportIsoToPath.agda-libraries

Alternative approach

Use`Equiv`/`ua`(univalence) instead of`Iso`, and reserve`Iso`for computational extraction

If your equivalences are naturallyEquiv(because you want univalence-friendly composition and “proof relevance”), use:

  • pathToEquiv : A ≡ B → A ≃ Band univalenceua : A ≃ B → A ≡ B(Cubical foundations).agda-librariesagda-libraries
  • Then convertEquivtoIsoonly at the boundary where you need concretefun/inv.

This tends to scale better when your semantic graph layer introduces truncations/quotients (e.g., quotienting by “alias” records or by “same person” merges).

Use SIP (Structure Identity Principle) to turn “structure-preserving renaming” directly into paths

If you model “genealogy with structure” asTypeWithStrand prove “renaming preserves structure”, SIP yields a Path between structured types, which you can then feed intopathToIso. The Cubical SIP patterns are explicitly used to derive paths between different implementations of thesame abstract structure.agda-libraries

In genealogy terms, SIP is a clean way to say:

“Reindexing people IDs is not just an Iso of carriers; it’s an equality of genealogical structures.”

Related GitHub repositories and references

  • Cubical Agda library(the source ofIso,isoToPath,pathToIso,transport, univalence, etc.).GitHub
  • Agda (compiler / language), relevant for running Cubical Agda and the--cubicalmode.Agda Documentation
  • FamilySearch GEDCOMresources / specification hosting (and related repos under FamilySearch).GitHub+1
  • agda-categories(useful if you decide to model semantic graphs categorically and use categoricalpathToIsopatterns).GitHub
  • FamilySearch GEDCOM tooling / ecosystems(Java / GEDCOM-related codebases you may want for interoperability tests).GitHub+1

If you tell me which exact “semantic graph” shape you want (e.g.,relations as sets vs adjacency lists vs indexed families), I can refactor the coreDatasetrecord so theIndiRecIso-sec/FamRecIso-secproofs become fully explicit (no postulates) while staying GEDCOM-faithful and keeping the samepathToIsorecipes.

entry.title: pagenotes splitter: csv2files
entry.content:
pagenotes.app .csv to separated .txt files

#!/usr/bin/env bash set -euo pipefail # split_pagenotes_csv.sh # # Input: CSV with 4 quoted fields per record: # "<url>","<timestamp>","<note text possibly with newlines>","<flag>" # # Output: one .txt per record in /home/rangelma/bash-csv/ # - filename derived from field 1 (URL) to match your examples: # * strip http:// or https:// # * split on '/' and join with '>' # * drop empty trailing segments (so trailing '/' doesn't create a trailing '>') # * sanitize unsafe characters to '_' # * append ".txt" # - file content is exactly field 3 (preserving internal newlines) usage() { cat <<'USAGE' Usage: split_pagenotes_csv.sh /path/to/notes.csv Writes files to: /home/rangelma/bash-csv/ USAGE } if [[ ${1:-} == "-h" || ${1:-} == "--help" ]]; then usage exit 0 fi if [[ $# -ne 1 ]]; then echo "Error: expected exactly 1 argument (the .csv file)." >&2 usage >&2 exit 2 fi infile=$1 outdir="/home/rangelma/bash-csv" if [[ ! -f "$infile" ]]; then echo "Error: input file not found: $infile" >&2 exit 2 fi mkdir -p "$outdir" python3 - "$infile" "$outdir" <<'PY' import csv import os import re import sys from urllib.parse import urlsplit infile, outdir = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2] os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True) def url_to_filename(url: str) -> str: url = url.strip() # Remove scheme to match the examples try: parts = urlsplit(url) if parts.scheme: rest = parts.netloc + parts.path else: rest = re.sub(r'^[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9+.-]*://', '', url) except Exception: rest = re.sub(r'^[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9+.-]*://', '', url) # Convert path separators to '>' rest = rest.replace("\\", "/") rest = rest.replace("/", ">") # Drop empty trailing segments (e.g., trailing '/') rest = rest.strip(">") # Sanitize: keep letters/digits/._->- ; replace everything else with '_' rest = re.sub(r"[^A-Za-z0-9.\-_>]", "_", rest) if not rest: rest = "untitled" return rest + ".txt" with open(infile, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f: reader = csv.reader( f, delimiter=",", quotechar='"', doublequote=True, strict=False, ) for row_idx, row in enumerate(reader, start=1): # Expecting 4 fields; tolerate extra fields by using at least the first 3 if not row or len(row) < 3: continue url = row[0] note_text = row[2] filename = url_to_filename(url) path = os.path.join(outdir, filename) # Avoid overwriting if multiple records map to the same filename base, ext = os.path.splitext(path) final_path = path n = 1 while os.path.exists(final_path): final_path = f"{base}__{n}{ext}" n += 1 with open(final_path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as out: out.write(note_text) if note_text and not note_text.endswith("\n"): out.write("\n") PY echo "Done. Wrote files to: $outdir"

entry.title: cubical agda libs
entry.content:
cubical agda core libraries

-- SOURCE URL: `https://agda.github.io/cubical/Agda.Builtin.Cubical.Equiv.html` {-# OPTIONS --erased-cubical --safe --no-sized-types --no-guardedness #-} module Agda.Builtin.Cubical.Equiv where open import Agda.Primitive open import Agda.Builtin.Sigma open import Agda.Primitive.Cubical renaming (primINeg to ~_; primIMax to _∨_; primIMin to _∧_; primHComp to hcomp; primTransp to transp; primComp to comp; itIsOne to 1=1) open import Agda.Builtin.Cubical.Path open import Agda.Builtin.Cubical.Sub renaming (Sub to _[_↦_]) import Agda.Builtin.Cubical.HCompU as HCompU module Helpers = HCompU.Helpers open Helpers -- We make this a record so that isEquiv can be proved using -- copatterns. This is good because copatterns don't get unfolded -- unless a projection is applied so it should be more efficient. record isEquiv {ℓ ℓ'} {A : Set ℓ} {B : Set ℓ'} (f : A → B) : Set (ℓ ⊔ ℓ') where no-eta-equality field equiv-proof : (y : B) → isContr (fiber f y) open isEquiv public infix 4 _≃_ _≃_ : ∀ {ℓ ℓ'} (A : Set ℓ) (B : Set ℓ') → Set (ℓ ⊔ ℓ') A ≃ B = Σ (A → B) \ f → (isEquiv f) equivFun : ∀ {ℓ ℓ'} {A : Set ℓ} {B : Set ℓ'} → A ≃ B → A → B equivFun e = fst e -- Improved version of equivProof compared to Lemma 5 in CCHM. We put -- the (φ = i0) face in contr' making it be definitionally c in this -- case. This makes the computational behavior better, in particular -- for transp in Glue. equivProof : ∀ {la lt} (T : Set la) (A : Set lt) → (w : T ≃ A) → (a : A) → ∀ ψ (f : Partial ψ (fiber (w .fst) a)) → fiber (w .fst) a [ ψ ↦ f ] equivProof A B w a ψ fb = inS (contr' {A = fiber (w .fst) a} (w .snd .equiv-proof a) ψ fb) where contr' : ∀ {ℓ} {A : Set ℓ} → isContr A → (φ : I) → (u : Partial φ A) → A contr' {A = A} (c , p) φ u = hcomp (λ i → λ { (φ = i1) → p (u 1=1) i ; (φ = i0) → c }) c {-# BUILTIN EQUIV _≃_ #-} {-# BUILTIN EQUIVFUN equivFun #-} {-# BUILTIN EQUIVPROOF equivProof #-} module _ {ℓ : I → Level} (P : (i : I) → Set (ℓ i)) where private E : (i : I) → Set (ℓ i) E = λ i → P i ~E : (i : I) → Set (ℓ (~ i)) ~E = λ i → P (~ i) A = P i0 B = P i1 f : A → B f x = transp E i0 x g : B → A g y = transp ~E i0 y u : ∀ i → A → E i u i x = transp (λ j → E (i ∧ j)) (~ i) x v : ∀ i → B → E i v i y = transp (λ j → ~E ( ~ i ∧ j)) i y fiberPath : (y : B) → (xβ0 xβ1 : fiber f y) → xβ0 ≡ xβ1 fiberPath y (x0 , β0) (x1 , β1) k = ω , λ j → δ (~ j) where module _ (j : I) where private sys : A → ∀ i → PartialP (~ j ∨ j) (λ _ → E (~ i)) sys x i (j = i0) = v (~ i) y sys x i (j = i1) = u (~ i) x ω0 = comp ~E (sys x0) ((β0 (~ j))) ω1 = comp ~E (sys x1) ((β1 (~ j))) θ0 = fill ~E (sys x0) (inS (β0 (~ j))) θ1 = fill ~E (sys x1) (inS (β1 (~ j))) sys = λ {j (k = i0) → ω0 j ; j (k = i1) → ω1 j} ω = hcomp sys (g y) θ = hfill sys (inS (g y)) δ = λ (j : I) → comp E (λ i → λ { (j = i0) → v i y ; (k = i0) → θ0 j (~ i) ; (j = i1) → u i ω ; (k = i1) → θ1 j (~ i) }) (θ j) γ : (y : B) → y ≡ f (g y) γ y j = comp E (λ i → λ { (j = i0) → v i y ; (j = i1) → u i (g y) }) (g y) pathToisEquiv : isEquiv f pathToisEquiv .equiv-proof y .fst .fst = g y pathToisEquiv .equiv-proof y .fst .snd = sym (γ y) pathToisEquiv .equiv-proof y .snd = fiberPath y _ pathToEquiv : A ≃ B pathToEquiv .fst = f pathToEquiv .snd = pathToisEquiv
-- SOURCE URL: `https://agda.github.io/cubical/Cubical.Axiom.Omniscience.html` {-# OPTIONS --cubical --no-import-sorts #-} module Cubical.Axiom.Omniscience where open import Cubical.Foundations.Function open import Cubical.Foundations.HLevels open import Cubical.Foundations.Isomorphism open import Cubical.Foundations.Prelude open import Cubical.Foundations.Transport open import Cubical.Data.Bool renaming (Bool to 𝟚; Bool→Type to ⟨_⟩) open import Cubical.Data.Empty as Empty open import Cubical.Data.Sum as Sum open import Cubical.HITs.PropositionalTruncation as PT open import Cubical.Relation.Nullary private variable ℓ ℓ' : Level A : Type ℓ -- Lesser limited principle of omniscience -- -- If two decidable predicates cannot both be satisfied, we can -- determine that one predicate cannot be satisfied. LLPO : Type ℓ → Type ℓ LLPO A = ∀(P Q : A → 𝟚) → (∀ x y → ⟨ P x ⟩ → ⟨ Q y ⟩ → ⊥) → ∥ (∀ x → ¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) ⊎ (∀ y → ¬ ⟨ Q y ⟩) ∥₁ isPropLLPO : isProp (LLPO A) isPropLLPO = isPropΠ3 λ _ _ _ → squash₁ -- As above, but without ensuring propositionality LLPO∞ : Type ℓ → Type ℓ LLPO∞ A = ∀(P Q : A → 𝟚) → (∀ x y → ⟨ P x ⟩ → ⟨ Q y ⟩ → ⊥) → (∀ x → ¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) ⊎ (∀ y → ¬ ⟨ Q y ⟩) LLPO∞→LLPO : LLPO∞ A → LLPO A LLPO∞→LLPO llpo' P Q ¬both = ∣ llpo' P Q ¬both ∣₁ -- Weak limited principle of omniscience -- -- It is decidable whether or not a decidable predicate never holds. WLPO : Type ℓ → Type ℓ WLPO A = ∀(P : A → 𝟚) → Dec (∀ x → ¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) WLPO' : Type ℓ → Type ℓ WLPO' A = ∀(P : A → 𝟚) → Dec (P ≡ const false) isPropWLPO : isProp (WLPO A) isPropWLPO = isPropΠ λ P → isPropDec (isPropΠ λ x → isProp¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) isPropWLPO' : isProp (WLPO' A) isPropWLPO' = isPropΠ λ P → isPropDec (isSet→ isSetBool P (const false)) module WLPO≃ where points : (P : A → 𝟚) → P ≡ const false → ∀ x → ¬ ⟨ P x ⟩ points P p x = subst (λ Q → ⟨ Q x ⟩) p total : (P : A → 𝟚) → (∀ x → ¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) → P ≡ const false total P never i x with P x | never x ... | false | _ = false ... | true | ¬⊤ = Empty.rec {A = true ≡ false} (¬⊤ _) i open Iso total≡points : ∀(P : A → 𝟚) → (P ≡ const false) ≡ (∀ x → ¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) total≡points P = isoToPath λ where .fun → points P .inv → total P .sec never → isPropΠ (λ x → isProp¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) _ never .ret α≡f → isSet→ isSetBool P (const false) _ α≡f WLPO≡WLPO' : WLPO A ≡ WLPO' A WLPO≡WLPO' {A = A} i = (P : A → 𝟚) → Dec (WLPO≃.total≡points P (~ i)) WLPO→LLPO∞ : WLPO A → LLPO∞ A WLPO→LLPO∞ {A = A} womn P Q ¬both with womn P ... | yes ∀¬P = inl ∀¬P ... | no ¬∀¬P = inr ∀¬Q where ∀¬Q : ∀ y → ¬ ⟨ Q y ⟩ ∀¬Q y Qy = ¬∀¬P (λ x Px → ¬both x y Px Qy) -- Limited principle of omniscience -- -- Either a decidable predicate never holds, or it does LPO : Type ℓ → Type ℓ LPO A = ∀(P : A → 𝟚) → (∀ x → ¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) ⊎ ∥ Σ[ x ∈ A ] ⟨ P x ⟩ ∥₁ LPO→WLPO : LPO A → WLPO A LPO→WLPO omn P with omn P ... | inl ∀¬P = yes ∀¬P ... | inr ∃P = no λ ∀¬P → PT.rec isProp⊥ (uncurry ∀¬P) ∃P -- As above, but without truncation. LPO∞ : Type ℓ → Type ℓ LPO∞ A = ∀(P : A → 𝟚) → (∀ x → ¬ ⟨ P x ⟩) ⊎ (Σ[ x ∈ A ] ⟨ P x ⟩) LPO∞→LPO : LPO∞ A → LPO A LPO∞→LPO omn P = Sum.map (idfun _) ∣_∣₁ (omn P)
-- SOURCE URL: `https://agda.github.io/cubical/Cubical.Categories.Category.Base` module Cubical.Categories.Category.Base where open import Cubical.Foundations.Prelude open import Cubical.Foundations.HLevels open import Cubical.Foundations.Equiv open import Cubical.Foundations.Powerset open import Cubical.Data.Sigma private variable ℓ ℓ' : Level -- Categories with hom-sets record Category ℓ ℓ' : Type (ℓ-suc (ℓ-max ℓ ℓ')) where -- TODO: document the impetus for this change no-eta-equality field ob : Type ℓ Hom[_,_] : ob → ob → Type ℓ' id : ∀ {x} → Hom[ x , x ] _⋆_ : ∀ {x y z} (f : Hom[ x , y ]) (g : Hom[ y , z ]) → Hom[ x , z ] ⋆IdL : ∀ {x y} (f : Hom[ x , y ]) → id ⋆ f ≡ f ⋆IdR : ∀ {x y} (f : Hom[ x , y ]) → f ⋆ id ≡ f ⋆Assoc : ∀ {x y z w} (f : Hom[ x , y ]) (g : Hom[ y , z ]) (h : Hom[ z , w ]) → (f ⋆ g) ⋆ h ≡ f ⋆ (g ⋆ h) isSetHom : ∀ {x y} → isSet Hom[ x , y ] -- composition: alternative to diagramatic order _∘_ : ∀ {x y z} (g : Hom[ y , z ]) (f : Hom[ x , y ]) → Hom[ x , z ] g ∘ f = f ⋆ g ⟨_⟩⋆⟨_⟩ : {x y z : ob} {f f' : Hom[ x , y ]} {g g' : Hom[ y , z ]} → f ≡ f' → g ≡ g' → f ⋆ g ≡ f' ⋆ g' ⟨ ≡f ⟩⋆⟨ ≡g ⟩ = cong₂ _⋆_ ≡f ≡g infixr 9 _⋆_ infixr 9 _∘_ open Category -- Helpful syntax/notation _[_,_] : (C : Category ℓ ℓ') → (x y : C .ob) → Type ℓ' _[_,_] = Hom[_,_] _End[_] : (C : Category ℓ ℓ') → (x : C .ob) → Type ℓ' C End[ x ] = C [ x , x ] -- Needed to define this in order to be able to make the subsequence syntax declaration seq' : ∀ (C : Category ℓ ℓ') {x y z} (f : C [ x , y ]) (g : C [ y , z ]) → C [ x , z ] seq' = _⋆_ infixl 15 seq' syntax seq' C f g = f ⋆⟨ C ⟩ g -- composition comp' : ∀ (C : Category ℓ ℓ') {x y z} (g : C [ y , z ]) (f : C [ x , y ]) → C [ x , z ] comp' = _∘_ infixr 16 comp' syntax comp' C g f = g ∘⟨ C ⟩ f -- Isomorphisms ℓ' CatIso C x y = Σ[ f ∈ C [ x , y ] ] isIso C f CatIso≡ : {C : Category ℓ ℓ'}{x y : C .ob}(f g : CatIso C x y) → f .fst ≡ g .fst → f ≡ g CatIso≡ f g = Σ≡Prop isPropIsIso -- `constructor` of CatIso isiso inv sec ret idCatIso : {C : Category ℓ ℓ'} {x : C .ob} → CatIso C x x idCatIso {C = C} = C .id , isiso (C .id) (C .⋆IdL (C .id)) (C .⋆IdL (C .id)) isSet-CatIso : {C : Category ℓ ℓ'} → ∀ x y → C} p = J (λ z _ → CatIso C _ z) idCatIso p pathToIso-refl : {C : Category ℓ ℓ'} {x : C .ob} → pathToIso {C = C} {x} refl ≡ idCatIso pathToIso-refl {C = C} {x} = JRefl (λ z _ → CatIso C x z) (idCatIso) renaming ( fiber to fiberPath ; isEquiv to isEquivPath ; _≃_ to EquivPath ; equivFun to equivFunPath ; isPropIsEquiv to isPropIsEquivPath ) hiding ( equivCtr ; equivIsEquiv ) open import
